ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper does not introduce an entirely new synthesis objective (it reuses the Gram-matrix framework of Gatys et al. 2015) but provides a notable and surprising new perspective: that a single-layer CNN with random (or simple) multi-scale filters can often match VGG-based multi-layer, pretrained models (see Contributions and Results). It systematically compares many filter types/sizes (Sec.2–6), proposes a quantitative VGG-based evaluation, and discusses theoretical implications for MaxEnt/finite-size effects, clearly differentiating its claims from prior work. This is a non-trivial, empirically-driven advance that would likely inform and surprise experts, but it is primarily an illuminating simplification rather than a wholly novel algorithmic framework.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claim—that single-layer CNNs with random filters can produce high-quality texture synthesis—is clearly stated and supported by extensive experiments across filter types/sizes (sec.2–6) and by reasonable mathematical definitions (Gram matrices and normalized distances, eqs.1–3). The mathematical parts are simple and correct, and the experimental design includes relevant baselines (Gatys VGG, Portilla & Simoncelli) and many ablations (multi-/single-scale, filter counts). However, the evaluation relies primarily on a VGG-based loss (sec.5) and qualitative images without psychophysical/human studies or statistical tests, so the perceptual claims are not rigorously validated and the metric could bias comparisons. Overall the conclusions are justified for the scope of texture synthesis given the presented evidence, but lack of human evaluation and formal statistical analysis are the main minor shortcomings.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is overall well organized with a logical flow (Introduction → model description in Sections 2–4 → evaluation and results → discussion) and the main contributions are stated explicitly and early in the Introduction (bullet list). The notation is mostly consistent and well defined (e.g. Gram matrix Gij in Section 3 and the distance definitions in (2),(3)), though there are minor symbol/style inconsistencies (e.g. use of G` in (6) and occasional awkward inline symbols). Figures and examples are frequently referenced and seem informative, but the provided text lacks the actual figures and the manuscript contains formatting issues (e.g. the footnote “average- instead of max-pooling1.” and extensive duplicated/mangled text in the Appendix) that hurt presentation. Overall writing is clear and readable, but these formatting/duplication errors and a few small grammatical awkwards justify a minor deduction.
